comment:
v3 narrowed scan on the pre-table gap. v1 (64 B @ 4 B step) and v2 (16 KB @ 1024 B step) both found no tap-tempo correlation; v2 confirmed the read mechanism works (positions 2/8/12/13 louder, others quieter, no BPM movement anywhere). v2 sampled 0xc009c000 alone before jumping past the state[31] table to 0xc009c400, leaving 0xc009c004..0xc009c19c (≈410 B immediately before the state[31] table) untouched. Global firmware data clusters near per-slot tables, so this gap is the highest-probability unexplored region.

v3: Addr knob (0..15) maps to *(0xc009c000 + 32*N). 16 positions × 32-byte step = covers 0xc009c000..0xc009c1e0 — the entire gap plus the first 64 B of the state[31] table as a cross-check.

No handler patches: state[31] lookup table and state[7] postbox both intact. The TAP UI (pedal_flags=0x28 on the Addr slot) gives us a way to change BPM and look for memory that tracks it.

Test plan:
  1. Load probe, unbypass. Sweep through 0..15 — expect mixed loudness as before.
  2. At each position, tap tempo at ~120 BPM then ~180 BPM. If gain CHANGES with tap tempo at any knob setting, that 32-byte block contains BPM storage. Note the knob index.
  3. A follow-up probe narrows to 4-byte step inside the hit block.
  4. If NO position tracks BPM, BPM is not clustered with the state[31] table. Pivot probe to 0x11f03xxx per-slot state or 0xc00fxxxx firmware globals.

params.name: Addr
params.flags_comment: 0x28 — the Pattern B tempo-sync bit. SyncProbe v1 confirmed this slot is the trigger for the pedal's TAP UI: with this flag, clicking the left knob exposes the tap-tempo button, which is the whole mechanism we need to drive BPM changes for this probe.
params.comment: Knob 0..15 selects which 4-byte word in the firmware-RAM scan window the audio function reads. The TAP UI lets the user tap a tempo while the chosen address is sampled.
